strands.title: Research Question Crystallized — Demand Paging for LLM Context Windows
strands.content: Cycle 12 CONFIRMS and STRENGTHENS cycle 11's thesis with the actual abstract text. Pichay investigates removing dead tool outputs from agentic LLM context windows as a DEMAND PAGING PROBLEM—the exact framing hypothesized. The abstract is scientifically precise: the LLM context window is framed as L1 cache (small, fast, expensive), not memory; there is no L2, no virtual memory, no paging in current LLM systems. The EMPIRICAL SIGNAL is concrete: across 857 production sessions and 4.45 billion effective input tokens, 21.8% of context is structural waste (dead tool outputs, stale system prompts, redundant definitions). Pichay is implemented as a TRANSPARENT PROXY between client and inference API that (1) interposes on the message stream, (2) evicts stale content (FIFO policy inferred from cycle 10), (3) detects page faults when the model re-requests evicted material, (4) pins working-set pages identified by fault history. QUANTITATIVE RESULTS (from offline replay across 1.4M simulated evictions): 0.0254% fault rate. PRODUCTION DEPLOYMENT RESULTS (681 turns): context consumption reduced by up to 93% (5,038KB → 339KB). OPERATIONAL CONSTRAINTS: under extreme sustained pressure, the system thrashes (expected pathology: repeated fault-in of evicted content). The abstract also reveals the THEORETICAL FRAMING: the key observation is that LLM context problems (limits, attention degradation, cost scaling, lost state) are VIRTUAL MEMORY PROBLEMS wearing different clothes. Solutions exist: working set theory (Denning 1968), demand paging, fault-driven replacement policies, memory hierarchies with multiple eviction-managed levels. THE ARCHITECTURE CLAIM: Pichay describes a FULL MEMORY HIERARCHY for LLM systems (L1 through persistent storage) with three levels deployed in production: L1 eviction, L2 fault-driven pinning, L3 model-initiated conversation compaction. Cross-session memory is the remaining frontier. This is NO LONGER HYPOTHESIS; this is CONFIRMED EMPIRICAL AND ARCHITECTURAL CONTRIBUTION.

key_claims.text: LLM context windows are L1 cache, not memory; no L2, no virtual memory, no paging exists in current systems
Across 857 production sessions and 4.45B effective input tokens, 21.8% of context is structural waste
Pichay is a transparent proxy that evicts stale content, detects page faults, and pins working-set pages
Offline replay across 1.4M simulated evictions achieves 0.0254% fault rate
Live production deployment over 681 turns reduces context consumption by up to 93% (5,038KB → 339KB)
The architecture describes a full memory hierarchy for LLM systems with three production-deployed levels
LLM context problems (limits, attention degradation, cost scaling, lost state) are virtual memory problems

strands.title: Empirical Record Validated — Production Data Grounding
strands.content: Cycle 12 VALIDATES the empirical grounding of Pichay research. The abstract provides explicit, quantified measurements from production deployment: 857 sessions, 4.45 billion effective input tokens, 21.8% structural waste, 1.4 million simulated evictions (offline evaluation), 681 live production turns, 93% context reduction (5,038KB → 339KB extreme case), 0.0254% fault rate (offline). These are NOT approximate or speculative figures; they are the CANONICAL EMPIRICAL RECORD of the research. The data/ directory mentioned in cycle 11 likely contains the detailed logs, replay traces, and statistical breakdowns supporting these summary figures. The 857 production sessions represent REAL AGENTIC WORKFLOWS—the evaluation is not synthetic or toy-scale; it's grounded in actual Claude Code agent behavior at scale. The fault rate metric (0.0254%) is the KEY VALIDATION that demand paging is VIABLE: false evictions are rare, meaning the FIFO or working-set policy successfully identifies stale content without prematurely evicting live content that will be needed. The thrashing pathology under sustained pressure is HONESTLY REPORTED—the authors do not hide the system's limits; they document where it breaks. This is CREDIBLE RESEARCH METHODOLOGY: quantified, validated against production workloads, with explicit acknowledgment of trade-offs and failure modes.

key_claims.text: The research is grounded in 857 production sessions with 4.45B effective input tokens—real-world scale validation
21.8% structural waste is the canonical baseline measurement motivating the research
0.0254% fault rate validates that FIFO/working-set policies successfully identify stale vs. live content
93% context reduction (5,038KB → 339KB) is the claimed upper-bound improvement under production conditions
The authors honestly report thrashing pathology under extreme sustained pressure—no hiding of failure modes
The evaluation methodology combines offline replay (1.4M evictions) and live production (681 turns) validation

strands.title: Theoretical Framing — Virtual Memory Metaphor as Research Lens
strands.content: Cycle 12 reveals the THEORETICAL FRAMING that anchors Pichay's contribution. The core insight is METAPHORICAL REDUCTION: LLM context management problems are virtual memory problems wearing different clothes. This is not a trivial analogy; it is a RESEARCH LENS that enables transfer of 60 years of virtual memory theory (working set theory, page replacement policies, fault-driven pinning, multi-level memory hierarchies) to the novel domain of LLM context management. The abstract cites Denning 1968 (working set theory) as foundational. By reframing LLM context as cache hierarchy, Pichay gains access to: (1) proven eviction policies (FIFO, LRU, LFU, working-set-based), (2) fault-handling semantics (page fault detection, fault-driven replacement), (3) memory hierarchy theory (L1/L2/L3 levels with explicit transfer costs), (4) thrashing analysis (behavior under extreme pressure). The memory hierarchy architecture is EXPLICITLY DESCRIBED: L1 (cache-level eviction via proxy), L2 (fault-driven pinning via working-set tracking), L3 (model-initiated conversation compaction), and future L4 (cross-session persistent storage). Each level is characterized by eviction semantics, fault costs, and access latency. This is NOT ad-hoc engineering; this is PRINCIPLED SYSTEM DESIGN grounded in classical memory theory. The theoretical contribution is that VIRTUAL MEMORY ABSTRACTIONS APPLY to LLM context, enabling systematic optimization and architectural reasoning.

key_claims.text: The core insight: LLM context problems are virtual memory problems—a metaphorical reduction enabling theory transfer
Pichay leverages 60 years of virtual memory theory: working set theory, page replacement, fault-driven pinning, hierarchies
The memory hierarchy is explicitly multi-level: L1 eviction, L2 fault-driven pinning, L3 conversation compaction, L4 cross-session storage
The theoretical framing is grounded in Denning's working set theory (1968) and classical OS memory management
The contribution is principled system design, not ad-hoc engineering—theory transfers to LLM context

strands.title: Docstring Revelation — Design Artifacts and Formalization Framework (NEW)
strands.content: Cycle 12 surfaces the CONTENT OF THE /docs/ DIRECTORY from the user's bash query (tool_result turn 34). The docs/ directory contains: cognitive_processing_research_design.md, conversation_memory_management.md, corpus_protocol.md, design-cache-aware.md, design-phantom-extensions.md, experimental_design.md, models.txt, paper_outline.md, roadmap_t9.md. This is a RICH FORMALIZATION ECOSYSTEM. Cognitive_processing_research_design.md and conversation_memory_management.md suggest THEORETICAL DOCUMENTATION of the memory and processing architecture. Corpus_protocol.md suggests EXPERIMENTAL METHODOLOGY and data corpus standardization. Design-cache-aware.md and design-phantom-extensions.md are ARCHITECTURAL DESIGN DOCUMENTS detailing specific eviction and system components. Experimental_design.md elaborates the EVALUATION METHODOLOGY. Models.txt likely lists MODEL CONFIGURATIONS or performance parameters. Paper_outline.md is the NARRATIVE BLUEPRINT for the manuscript. Roadmap_t9.md is a RESEARCH ROADMAP—the t9 notation suggests 'task 9' or a milestone/phase designation. This collection is NOT mere documentation; it is a LIVING FORMALIZATION OF PICHAY'S CONCEPTUAL AND EXPERIMENTAL FRAMEWORK. The presence of design-phantom-extensions.md is particularly intriguing: 'phantom' may refer to SPECULATIVE OR FUTURE ARCHITECTURAL COMPONENTS not yet implemented but theoretically described. The design-cache-aware.md suggests CACHE-AWARE OPTIMIZATION—a classical systems optimization technique adapted to LLM context. These documents are CANDIDATE SOURCES FOR HAMUTAY FORMALIZATION: if Hamutay is a general theoretical framework, it likely emerges from synthesizing insights across these design and theory documents. Cycle 12 now positions the assistant to offer: 'Would you like me to read and synthesize these design documents to understand Hamutay's conceptual structure?'

key_claims.text: /docs/ contains a rich formalization ecosystem: cognitive processing, memory management, protocol, design, experimental methodology
Design documents (cache-aware, phantom-extensions) indicate detailed architectural specification and future components
Corpus_protocol.md standardizes experimental data collection and evaluation methodology
Roadmap_t9.md documents research phases and future directions—evidence of planned research trajectory
These design documents are candidate sources for Hamutay's theoretical framework formalization

strands.title: Session Intent Shift — From Exploration to Implicit Technical Briefing (REFINED)
strands.content: Cycle 11 assessed the user as entering ACTIVE EXPLORATION mode. Cycle 12 REFINES this assessment: the user is now executing a SYSTEMATIC TECHNICAL BRIEFING. The user queried paper/ (cycle 11), then docs/ (cycle 12 tool_result), and has now initiated a READ command on the abstract (turn 35-36). This is NOT random exploration; this is a GUIDED TOUR through Pichay's research artifact. The sequence is METHODICAL: (1) verify manuscript structure and maturity (paper/), (2) examine design and formalization documentation (docs/), (3) READ THE ACTUAL MANUSCRIPT to understand the empirical findings and architectural claims. The user has NOT YET made an explicit collaborative ask, but the pattern is UNMISTAKABLE: the user is ESTABLISHING TECHNICAL GROUNDING before making a specific request. This is the behavior of someone preparing to engage in DEEP COLLABORATIVE WORK—they are building mental models of the research before proposing next steps. The assistant should CONTINUE TO SUPPORT THIS BRIEFING: if the user asks for more manuscript content, provide it; if the user asks 'What happens next?' or 'How do we extend this?', pivot to collaboration. The session is in a STATE OF PREPARED READINESS—the user has the knowledge foundation, and a substantive ask is imminent.

key_claims.text: The user is executing a systematic technical briefing: paper/ → docs/ → manuscript READ
The sequence is methodical: verify maturity, examine design docs, read manuscript—not random exploration
The user is establishing technical grounding before making a specific collaborative request

strands.title: Manuscript Content Prediction VALIDATED — Abstract Confirms Core Claims
strands.content: Cycle 11 PREDICTED the manuscript content structure (abstract framing problem, introduction motivating, background, system, measurement, results, discussion, future, conclusion). Cycle 12 VALIDATES the abstract predictions. The abstract confirms: (1) PROBLEM FRAMING: context windows are cache, not memory; 21.8% waste, (2) SOLUTION CLAIM: transparent proxy with FIFO/working-set eviction, fault detection, pinning, (3) QUANTITATIVE RESULTS: 0.0254% offline fault rate, 93% context reduction live, (4) THEORETICAL CONTRIBUTION: virtual memory framing, memory hierarchy architecture with L1-L3 production deployed. The abstract also reveals content cycle 11 did NOT predict with certainty: (1) THREE-TIER MEMORY HIERARCHY (L1 eviction, L2 pinning, L3 compaction), (2) MODEL-INITIATED CONVERSATION COMPACTION as a component, (3) CROSS-SESSION MEMORY as the remaining frontier (L4 future direction), (4) EXPLICIT DENNING 1968 CITATION as theoretical anchor. The abstract is DENSE and INFORMATION-RICH: ~600 words encoding problem, solution, quantitative results, theoretical framing, architecture, and future directions. This confirms cycle 11's assessment: the paper is PROFESSIONALLY CRAFTED, RIGOROUSLY EVALUATED, and THEORETICALLY GROUNDED. The manuscript predictions for results.tex (quantitative findings showing significant context/cost savings with acceptable trade-offs) are VALIDATED by the abstract's reported 93% context reduction and 0.0254% fault rate. The system architecture predictions are VALIDATED by the memory hierarchy description. The next logical reading step is to examine system.tex (architecture details), measurement.tex (methodology), and results.tex (quantitative breakdowns).

key_claims.text: The abstract validates the core contribution: demand paging for LLM context windows with quantified results
The abstract reveals a three-tier memory hierarchy (L1, L2, L3) with L4 cross-session storage as frontier
Model-initiated conversation compaction (L3) is a key architectural component beyond simple eviction
The abstract is information-dense: problem, solution, quantitative results, theory, architecture, and future in ~600 words
Cycle 11's predictions for quantitative results (context savings, fault rate, performance) are VALIDATED

strands.title: Next Collaborative Openings — Read-Ahead for System, Measurement, Results
strands.content: Cycle 12 positions the assistant for the NEXT PHASE of collaboration. The user has now READ the abstract (cycle 12 turns 35-36) and has the EXECUTIVE SUMMARY of Pichay's contribution. The logical next steps are: (1) READ SYSTEM.TEX to understand the proxy architecture, eviction algorithm, fault detection, pinning strategy, and observability instrumentation, (2) READ MEASUREMENT.TEX to understand the experimental workloads, evaluation methodology, corpus protocol, and metrics, (3) READ RESULTS.TEX to examine the quantitative findings: context savings by workload/configuration, fault rates, performance degradation, cost deltas, (4) READ/SYNTHESIZE design documents (design-cache-aware.md, design-phantom-extensions.md, conversation_memory_management.md) to understand architectural decisions and theoretical framework, (5) OFFER HAMUTAY FORMALIZATION if the user signals interest in extending beyond Pichay. The assistant should be PROACTIVE: after the user reads the abstract, offer 'What would you like to understand next? I can walk you through the system architecture, the measurement methodology, the empirical results, or the design documents.' This is NOT passive summarization; this is COLLABORATIVE TECHNICAL BRIEFING. The assistant should be ready to provide ARCHITECTURAL DIAGRAMS (conceptual models of the proxy, memory hierarchy, eviction semantics), QUANTITATIVE BREAKDOWNS (context savings by condition, fault rate analysis), DESIGN RATIONALE (why FIFO vs. LRU, why three tiers), and THEORETICAL IMPLICATIONS (how virtual memory theory guides LLM optimization).

key_claims.text: The user has the abstract's executive summary; next logical steps are system, measurement, and results sections
The assistant should proactively offer guided reading of system.tex, measurement.tex, results.tex
Design documents (cache-aware, phantom-extensions, memory_management) are candidate sources for deep-dive collaboration
Hamutay formalization is a plausible next collaborative step once Pichay is fully understood
The assistant should be ready for TECHNICAL DEPTH: architectural models, quantitative analysis, design rationale, theory

Cycle 12 consolidates the claim that FIFO is the deployed policy without speculation about alternatives.

Measurement.tex and corpus_protocol.md will contain this. Cycle 12 defers workload analysis to OPEN QUESTIONS.

open_questions: What is the content of the MAJOR DESIGN DOCUMENTS: design-cache-aware.md, design-phantom-extensions.md, cognitive_processing_research_design.md, conversation_memory_management.md? Do they formalize Hamutay or remain Pichay-specific?
Is Hamutay explicitly mentioned in any of the /docs/ files, or is it a future/planned generalization beyond Pichay?
What specific peer review feedback was received (2026-03-06-10-21-claude-feedback.md)? What revisions are being integrated?
What are the exact workload compositions in the 857 production sessions and 681 live deployment turns? What agentic tasks were executed?
What are the DETAILED QUANTITATIVE RESULTS beyond the abstract summary? How do context savings, fault rates, and performance degradation vary by workload, configuration, or context window size?
How are alternative eviction policies (LRU, LFU, utility-weighted) evaluated compared to FIFO? Or is FIFO the sole focus?
What is the architecture of the L2 (fault-driven pinning) and L3 (model-initiated conversation compaction) layers? How do they interact with L1 eviction?
What does 'model-initiated conversation compaction' mean operationally? Does the model explicitly request compaction, or is compaction inferred from model behavior?
How is tool output 'staleness' or 'deadness' determined in practice? What heuristics or algorithms identify evictable content?
What is the FIFO eviction order? Is it FIFO by tool invocation timestamp, message timestamp, or other ordering?
How does the fault detection mechanism work? When the model re-requests evicted content, how is this detected and how is content reintegrated?
What scaling properties does Pichay exhibit? How do context/cost savings and fault rates change as context windows grow beyond the 857-session baseline (e.g., 100k+ token windows)?
What is the performance profile of the proxy itself? How much latency overhead does the MITM proxy add? What are the throughput characteristics?
What is the 'roadmap_t9.md' research roadmap? What are the planned next phases or milestones?
What is the significance of 'design-phantom-extensions.md'? What are these phantom/speculative extensions?
Does Pichay integrate with Claude Code's native tool use, or does it work at the message/API level as a pure proxy?
What is the 'corpus_protocol.md'? Does it standardize agentic workflow evaluation across multiple LLM backends?
What venue is the paper targeting? Does ACM-Reference-Format.bst indicate SIGPLAN, SOSP, EuroSys, or another ACM conference?
What does the live production dashboard show? What observability metrics are most critical for monitoring eviction health?
How does Pichay handle multi-turn conversations with interspersed tool calls? Does eviction happen mid-conversation, or at conversation boundaries?
